title: LDR / STR: Touching Memory the ARM Way
lesson:
ARM is a load-store machine, just like MIPS and the LC-3. Arithmetic instructions only touch registers; the only way to read or write memory is through LDR (Load Register) and STR (Store Register). The syntax uses square brackets to mean 'the memory at this address': 'ldr x0, [x1]' loads the word x1 points at into x0. You can also add an offset: 'ldr x0, [x1, #4]' loads the word four bytes past x1.

The '.data' section gives you a place to put constants in memory. On our teaching machine, the .data section starts at address 0x1000, so 'mov x1, #0x1000' points x1 at the very first byte of .data.
prompt: Load the value 30 from a .word stored at the start of .data, and print it.
starterCode:
.data
x:      .word 30
.text
        mov x1, #0x1000      // address of x (first word of .data)
        ldr x0, [x1]         // x0 = the word at that address (= 30)
        mov x16, #1
        svc #0
        mov x16, #93
        svc #0
hints: mov x1, #0x1000 puts the .data section's base address into x1.
ldr x0, [x1] reads the 32-bit word at that address into x0.
successText: The terminal must print 30, loaded from memory.
reference:
.data
x: .word 30
.text
mov x1, #0x1000
ldr x0, [x1]
mov x16, #1
svc #0
mov x16, #93
svc #0
